posts.title: SEO: The silent Engine of sustainable (organically) Business growth
posts.author: Camilla Andersson
posts.content_html:
Visibility is currency. You’ve invested time and resources into created your website to perfectly represents your business. It’s polished, professional and primed to convert visitors into customers. But here’s the crucial question: Are people actually finding it? Does it stands out in the crowd? Enter Search Engine Optimisation (SEO) – the unsung hero of digital marketing.

That’s where 𝗦earch 𝗘ngine 𝗢ptimisation (𝗦𝗘𝗢) becomes your best friend.

SEO isn’t just about climbing to the top of search results; it’s about ensuring your digital investment reaches your desired audience.

𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘄𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 • 68% of online experiences begin with a search engine • The first five organic results account for 67.60% of all clicks • 75% of users never scroll past the first page of search results

Now, factor in the rising tide of AI-powered searches. As technologies like ChatGPT and Claude Code redefine how people find information, being visible in these new searches is becoming increasingly crucial.

𝗢𝘂𝗿 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵: 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁-𝗗𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗦𝗘𝗢 𝘁𝗼 𝗳𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲-𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗼𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆

We are using advanced AI tools to analyse your website’s performance, uncovering opportunities (and fixes) to increase your visibility across both traditional and emerging search platforms.

With strategic SEO, we ensure your digital investment works hard for you, connecting your business with the right audience at the right time.

👩🏼‍💻 𝗪𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗮 𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗙𝗲𝗯𝗿𝘂𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗦𝗘𝗢 𝗔𝘂𝗱𝗶𝘁 𝗖𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗶𝗴𝗻. 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲’𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗴𝗲𝘁:
✨ 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗵𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗦𝗘𝗢 𝗔𝘂𝗱𝗶𝘁: 𝗔𝗜 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘀𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘄𝗲𝗯𝘀𝗶𝘁𝗲’𝘀 𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗦𝗘𝗢 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲.
✨ 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀: 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗲𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗮 𝗰𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗿 𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝗼𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝘂𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵 𝘃𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆.
✨ 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗤𝘂𝗼𝘁𝗲: 𝗙𝗼𝗿 𝗲𝗮𝗰𝗵 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻, 𝘄𝗲’𝗹𝗹 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝘃𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝗮 𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗼𝘁𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗶𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻.

Contact us for a discovery call: 📧 marten@angner.se / camilla@somestockholm.com

posts.title: WDS Session 4: Concept specifications and super prompting.
posts.author: Camilla Andersson
posts.content_html:
📅 Date: Wednesday, 11th February
⏰ Time: 17:00 – 19:00 CEST
📍 Location: Online
🎤 Host: Mårten Angner, founder of WDS

Whiteport Design Studio (WDS) is an end-to-end, AI-agent–driven design workflow where your design brilliance become the new code. Instead of wrestling fragile, one-off prompts or jumping straight into building, you shape clear intent, structure, and decisions into reusable product artifacts.

Our design files, sketches and concepts, will guide AI and human developers with precision, keep the project coherent when things change, and let designers stay in their strongest lane: making meaning, defining outcomes, and designing systems people actually want.

For designers everywhere, this matters because it turns “AI output” into a repeatable process. You get faster clarity, less rework, cleaner handoffs, and a way to ship without pretending you’re suddenly a full-time developer.However, This is a challenge for us designers!

This new world of AI is not particularly friendly to designers. AI lives where the code lives and we designers all have to make a great effort to expand and grow out of our visual comfort zone and become a little bit tech savvy. We know it is hard to grow, but don´t you worry. Whiteport has your back! Come online and we will help you get started just fine!

Date & time

Wednesday, 11th February on Google Meet
17:00-19:00 CEST

  • 08:00 – 10:00 PST (Los Angeles)
  • 09:00 – 11:00 MST (Denver)
  • 10:00 – 12:00 CST (Chicago)
  • 11:00 – 13:00 EST (New York)
  • 16:00 – 18:00 GMT (London)
  • 18:00 – 20:00 EET (Helsinki)

Sign-up

Missed Session 1?

Watch the recording here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TdujvNYI-3g

What is WDS, and why does it matter?

A quick recap of Sessions 1 & 2

In Session 1 we explored the shift that happens when you’re forced to explain your ideas, component by component, decision by decision. That’s where clarity appears. And when your product is generated from a clear specification, change becomes simple: you adjust the intent, reshuffle decisions, and regenerate.

In Session 2 we talked strategy, and why speed without thinking is just expensive wandering. We also touched on a very real detail: WDS lives in the same world as developers do, inside an IDE like Cursor or Windsurf, with Git as the backbone.

///
Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com

posts.title: Why the best AI coders think before they prompt
posts.author: Marten Angner
posts.content_html:
You’ve burned 3 hours and $50 in AI credits… and you’re further from done than when you started. Sound familiar?

Here’s the uncomfortable truth: the problem isn’t AI. It’s that you skipped strategy.

“You can’t walk two paths at the same time.” That’s the simplest explanation of strategy I’ve ever heard, and it came up during our latest WDS Session. We were diving into what strategy actually means – and why so many teams confuse it with something else entirely.

Strategy is the road you choose to reach your vision. Not the destination itself. Not the individual steps. The road.

Here’s the thing most people get wrong: You can’t have “strategies” – plural. You have one strategy. Sure, it can have different parts and components, but fundamentally it’s your singular path forward. The moment you try to pursue multiple conflicting strategies, you’re essentially standing still while pretending to move.

Think of it like climbing a mountain. Your vision is reaching the top. Your strategy is the specific route you’ve chosen to get there – maybe the north face, maybe the eastern trail. Your plan is the waypoints and milestones along that route.

The hidden value of strategic thinking

Here’s something that doesn’t get talked about enough: the act of strategic thinking is valuable in itself.

When your thinking is clearer and more structured, everything downstream improves. You spend your resources more effectively. You make better decisions faster. You stop wasting time on activities that don’t actually move you toward your goal.

In the age of AI agents and automation, this matters more than ever. Every minute you spend in productive, strategic AI sessions becomes exponentially more valuable when you actually know where you’re going.

The question isn’t whether you have a strategy. The question is whether your strategy is conscious and intentional – or just whatever path you happened to stumble onto.

The three levels of thinking: Why, How and What

One of the most powerful things you can do is separate your thinking into distinct levels. This isn’t just organizational tidiness – it fundamentally changes the quality of your decisions.

  • Why = Vision – The purpose, the destination, the reason you’re doing any of this in the first place
  • How = Strategy – The approach, the chosen path, the way you’ll get there
  • What = Tactics/Planning – The actions, the steps, the concrete things you’ll do today and tomorrow

Why does separating these matter so much?

  • Meetings become productive. When everyone knows which level you’re discussing, conversations stay focused. You reach alignment faster because you’re all talking about the same thing.
  • You spot opportunities for alignment. When tactics connect clearly to strategy, and strategy serves vision, every action moves you forward. Your team becomes genuinely productive, not just busy.
  • Adaptation becomes precise. When something needs adjustment, you know exactly which level to work on. Small course corrections at the tactical level, strategic pivots when the path needs changing.
  • Teams gain autonomy and momentum. When everyone understands the why and the how, they can figure out the what themselves. Decisions happen faster. People feel empowered.

Join the WDS Community

This article comes from insights shared during WDS Session 2: Strategy in Practice – one of our free, online sessions where designers learn to work smarter with AI agents.

WDS (Whiteport Design Studio) is a free and open-source AI agent module built for designers. It’s not about replacing your design process – it’s about amplifying it.

Join WDS Session 3: Let’s Get Going!

Sign up for WDS Session 3

social_posts.post_text:
3 hours. $50 in AI credits. And somehow you’re MORE lost than before. 😅

Sound familiar? Here’s the truth bomb: AI wasn’t the problem. You skipped the strategy.

Strategy = the road you choose.
Not the destination.
Not the steps.
The ROAD. 🛤️

And here’s the thing: you can only walk ONE road at a time. Multiple “strategies” = standing still while looking busy.

🏔️ Think mountain climbing:
• Vision = summit
• Strategy = your route
• Plan = where to rest

Change strategy only when:
✦ Your destination changes
✦ There’s an actual blockage (not just fear)

The secret? Strategic thinking itself is the superpower. Clear head = clear path = better everything. 🧠

Before your next AI session, ask:
✅ Can I explain my goal simply?
✅ Did I pick ONE approach?
✅ Do I know what I’m NOT doing?

No? Then you’re not ready to build. You’re ready to think. ☕

#Strategy #DesignStrategy #WDS #AIAgents #StrategicThinking #UXStrategy

Ever spent 3 hours fighting with AI tools… only to end up MORE confused than when you started? 😅

Yeah, we’ve all been there. And here’s the plot twist: the AI wasn’t the problem. You just skipped the thinking part.

Here’s the deal: Strategy is just the road you pick to get where you’re going. Not the destination. Not the to-do list. Just… the road.

And here’s where most of us mess up: you can’t walk two roads at once. (Trust me, I’ve tried. It’s called “spinning your wheels while looking busy.”)

Think of it like climbing a mountain 🏔️
→ Vision = that sweet summit view
→ Strategy = which trail you’re taking
→ Plan = where you’ll stop for snacks along the way

Why bother with all this thinking?

Because here’s the magic nobody talks about: the act of strategic thinking makes everything else easier. Seriously.

When you actually know where you’re going, you stop:
• Having meetings about meetings
• Building features nobody asked for
• Feeling busy but getting nowhere
• Arguing about stuff that doesn’t matter

And in the age of AI? This is HUGE. Clear thinking + AI tools = actual superpowers. Fuzzy thinking + AI tools = expensive confusion. 🤖

Quick sanity check before your next project:

✅ Can you explain your goal to a 10-year-old?
✅ Have you picked ONE approach (not three “options”)?
✅ Do you know what you’re NOT building?
✅ Is your first step small enough to actually finish?

If you’re nodding along, you’re ready to build. If you’re squinting at the screen… maybe grab a coffee and think first. ☕

The real question isn’t whether you have a strategy. It’s whether you CHOSE it… or just stumbled into it. 🤔

👇 Drop a 🔥 if you’ve ever been guilty of “strategy by accident”

#Strategy #DesignStrategy #AITools #WDS #RealTalk

You’ve burned 3 hours and $50 in AI credits… and you’re further from done than when you started.

Here’s the uncomfortable truth: the problem isn’t AI. It’s that you skipped strategy.

Strategy is the road you choose to reach your vision. Not the destination. Not the individual steps. The road.

Here’s what most teams get wrong: you can’t have “strategies” — plural. You have ONE strategy. The moment you pursue multiple conflicting directions, you’re standing still while pretending to move.

Think of climbing a mountain:
→ Vision = the summit
→ Strategy = your chosen route
→ Plan = the waypoints along the way

The Hidden Value of Strategy

Here’s what doesn’t get talked about enough: the act of strategic thinking is valuable in itself. 🧠

“The planning is everything, the plan is nothing.” Why? Because the work of thinking elevates you and your team. Strategy gives you the opportunity to think better — to predict the future and align your actions with it.

When your thinking is clearer and more structured, everything downstream improves. You spend resources more effectively. You make better decisions faster. You stop wasting time on activities that don’t actually move you forward.

In the age of AI agents and automation, this matters more than ever.

The Three Levels of Thinking

One of the most powerful things you can do is separate your thinking into distinct levels:

⭐ Why = Vision — The purpose, the destination, the reason you’re doing any of this
🧭 How = Strategy — The approach, the chosen path, the way you’ll get there
🔧 What = Tactics — The actions, the steps, the concrete things you’ll do today

Why does this matter?

• Meetings become productive — everyone knows which level you’re discussing
• You spot opportunities for alignment — tactics serve strategy, strategy serves vision
• Adaptation becomes precise — you know exactly which level needs adjustment
• Teams gain autonomy — when everyone understands the why and how, they figure out the what

Your Pre-AI Checklist

Before you open that AI chat and start burning credits, run through this:

Step 1: Lock in Your Vision
Ask: “What does success look like? Who benefits? Why does this matter?”
✅ Ready when: You can explain the end goal to a 10-year-old.

Step 2: Choose Your Strategy
Ask: “What’s the ONE approach? What am I NOT doing? What are my constraints?”
✅ Ready when: You’ve picked ONE path, not three “options to explore.”

Step 3: Define First Tactics
Ask: “What’s the first concrete step? What’s the smallest thing I can build to test this?”
✅ Ready when: Your first task fits in one AI session.

The Ready-to-Code Check:
☐ I can state my vision in one sentence
☐ I’ve chosen ONE strategy
☐ I know what I’m NOT building
☐ My first task is specific and small
☐ I know how I’ll recognize success

If you can’t check all boxes, you’re not ready to code. You’re ready to think.

👇 Links to the full article and upcoming WDS sessions in the first comment.

#Strategy #DesignStrategy #WDS #AIAgents #StrategicThinking #UXStrategy

😅 WDS Session 3: From “White Panic” to First Commit

Session 3 is where we connect, install, and get started with the WDS agents for real. This is the session for every designer who’s ever opened a code editor and felt that familiar blank-page dread. Not because you’re “bad at code”. But because designers are used to making meaning, not wrestling tools.

So we’ll do the unglamorous part together. The setup. The technical threshold. The first steps.
And we’ll do it in a room where nobody is trying to prove how “technical” they are.

✅ The payoff, Nothing less than mindblowing. What WDS will give you

  • A designer-first bridge into AI delivery: you stay focused on intent, structure, and user value while AI helps with implementation.
  • Less rework when things change: update the spec, not a pile of fragile generated code.
  • Clearer handoffs: your artifacts become a shared language between design, product, and dev.
  • Faster decisions: the process forces clarity early, so you stop guessing later.
  • Reusable patterns: turn your best thinking into templates you can use again and again.
  • More confidence in “technical” spaces: IDE, repos, and Git stop being scary and start being tools you can use.
  • Stronger product outcomes: because strategy and structure are baked in before anyone hits “build.”

➡️ What we’ll do together

  • Connect and prepare your environment for Whiteport Design Studio
  • Get the WDS agents running (the practical, real-world “hello world”)
  • Understand the moving parts: IDE, repo, version control, workflow
  • Make it feel… normal. Like just another design tool you haven’t met yet

Please come prepared with

  • Your GitHub username
  • An installed IDE: Cursor or Windsurf

Don’t be scared. You are among friends.
No one in this call “loves code”. We love design.
We’re just stretching a little, so we can design and ship awesome software with AI.

Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com
posts.content_raw:
📅 Date: Thursday, January 29th
⏰ Time: 17:00 – 19:00 CEST
📍 Location: Online
🎤 Host: Mårten Angner, founder of WDS

Whiteport Design Studio (WDS) is an end-to-end, AI-agent–driven design workflow where your design brilliance become the new code. Instead of wrestling fragile, one-off prompts or jumping straight into building, you shape clear intent, structure, and decisions into reusable product artifacts.

Our design files, sketches and concepts, will guide AI and human developers with precision, keep the project coherent when things change, and let designers stay in their strongest lane: making meaning, defining outcomes, and designing systems people actually want.

For designers everywhere, this matters because it turns “AI output” into a repeatable process. You get faster clarity, less rework, cleaner handoffs, and a way to ship without pretending you’re suddenly a full-time developer.However, This is a challenge for us designers!

This new world of AI is not particularly friendly to designers. AI lives where the code lives and we designers all have to make a great effort to expand and grow out of our visual comfort zone and become a little bit tech savvy. We know it is hard to grow, but don´t you worry. Whiteport has your back! Come online and we will help you get started just fine!

😅 WDS Session 3: From “White Panic” to First Commit

Session 3 is where we connect, install, and get started with the WDS agents for real. This is the session for every designer who’s ever opened a code editor and felt that familiar blank-page dread. Not because you’re “bad at code”. But because designers are used to making meaning, not wrestling tools.

[mailerlite_form form_id=6]

So we’ll do the unglamorous part together. The setup. The technical threshold. The first steps.
And we’ll do it in a room where nobody is trying to prove how “technical” they are.

✅ The payoff, Nothing less than mindblowing. What WDS will give you

  • A designer-first bridge into AI delivery: you stay focused on intent, structure, and user value while AI helps with implementation.
  • Less rework when things change: update the spec, not a pile of fragile generated code.
  • Clearer handoffs: your artifacts become a shared language between design, product, and dev.
  • Faster decisions: the process forces clarity early, so you stop guessing later.
  • Reusable patterns: turn your best thinking into templates you can use again and again.
  • More confidence in “technical” spaces: IDE, repos, and Git stop being scary and start being tools you can use.
  • Stronger product outcomes: because strategy and structure are baked in before anyone hits “build.”

➡️ What we’ll do together

  • Connect and prepare your environment for Whiteport Design Studio
  • Get the WDS agents running (the practical, real-world “hello world”)
  • Understand the moving parts: IDE, repo, version control, workflow
  • Make it feel… normal. Like just another design tool you haven’t met yet

Please come prepared with

  • Your GitHub username
  • An installed IDE: Cursor or Windsurf

Don’t be scared. You are among friends.
No one in this call “loves code”. We love design.
We’re just stretching a little, so we can design and ship awesome software with AI.

Date & time

29th January on Google Meet
17:00-19:00 CEST

  • 08:00 – 10:00 PST (Los Angeles)
  • 09:00 – 11:00 MST (Denver)
  • 10:00 – 12:00 CST (Chicago)
  • 11:00 – 13:00 EST (New York)
  • 16:00 – 18:00 GMT (London)
  • 18:00 – 20:00 EET (Helsinki)

Sign-up

[SIGN-UP LINK]

Missed Session 1?

Watch the recording here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TdujvNYI-3g

What is WDS, and why does it matter?

A quick recap of Sessions 1 & 2

In Session 1 we explored the shift that happens when you’re forced to explain your ideas, component by component, decision by decision. That’s where clarity appears. And when your product is generated from a clear specification, change becomes simple: you adjust the intent, reshuffle decisions, and regenerate.

In Session 2 we talked strategy, and why speed without thinking is just expensive wandering. We also touched on a very real detail: WDS lives in the same world as developers do, inside an IDE like Cursor or Windsurf, with Git as the backbone.

posts.title: The Prompt Burn is real!
posts.author: Marten Angner
posts.content_html:
“Prompt and burn” The new way we waste hard earned money for useless things and stay ignorant! Not thinking before you make things is a new kind of waste creeping into digital projects, and it sounds productive. Under the gies of release fast, we act reckless while the AI companies line their pockets!

You know the pattern: someone “just tests a few things” in an AI tool. Throws in more prompts. Iterates. Changes angle. Tries again. Eventually something looks right. Everyone’s happy.

Then reality arrives.

Requirements shift. The solution doesn’t hang together. The output was polished but not true. So you spin up the next round of prompts, and the next, and the next.

That’s prompt burn.

And it’s the same old villain in a new costume: scope creep, cloud sprawl, “just one more feature,” or the classic: “we’ll fix it next sprint.”

The difference now is that the waste feels creative and fast.

On the invoice, it’s still waste.

The problem isn’t that AI is expensive, it’s that the workflow becomes expensive

When prompting is unstructured, you often pay for three things at once:

  1. Compute: tokens burned, reruns, retries.
  2. Interpretation: human time spent figuring out what the AI actually meant.
  3. Rework: project cost when output doesn’t match business logic, user flows, or technical constraints.

That’s how teams end up in a paradox: it’s fast to get something, but slow to get something right.

BMAD: break work into atomic strategic units so AI can behave like a grown-up colleague

At its core, the BMAD method is the opposite of “prompt-and-pray.”

Instead of tossing a big fuzzy problem into a chat window, you break it down into atomic, strategic units that have:

  • a clear purpose
  • clear context
  • a clear output
  • clear quality criteria

It sounds simple, but the effect is brutal: AI gets the conditions it needs to deliver consistently, and you stop paying for it to hallucinate its way to a solution.

And when work is structured into reusable units, you can reuse them.

You get a system, not a one-off demo.

WDS: Whiteboard Design Studio makes the method practical

BMAD becomes truly powerful when it’s not just a concept, but a flow.

Whiteboard Design Studio (WDS) is the framework that takes you from:

intention → strategy → specification → execution

…without losing the human in the middle.

That means design teams don’t have to choose between “we shipped” and “it’s good.” You still work with goals, audience, priorities, and concepts, but you do it with an AI agent setup that:

  • asks the right questions in the right order
  • helps you make choices that actually connect
  • produces artifacts (briefs, specs, scenarios, requirements, copy frameworks) that teams can build from

The result: you move time from rework to direction.

There’s a little lie that still lives in tech: that speed alone wins.

But building a product in 30 minutes or 10 days often matters less than people want to believe.

What matters is:

  • the product solves the right problem
  • the team has a plan for success (not just a sprint plan)
  • the process and budget are sustainable
  • you can keep delivering without burning the team out or restarting every other week

WDS + BMAD is built for that.

Not for the demo.

For reality.

Okay, but how does it actually save time and money?

Here are the three most common ways cost drops:

1) Fewer do-overs
When intention, goals, and constraints are clear early, you avoid “build it twice” later.

2) Less prompt wear-and-tear
You work with reusable building blocks instead of reinventing the wheel in every new chat.

3) Better design–dev collaboration
Clear, connected specifications reduce friction, misunderstandings, and “we thought you meant…”

The smart choice going forward

If you want AI to save time and money, it’s not enough to “use AI.”

You need a way of working where AI:

  • can work in a structured way
  • can be checked and steered
  • can iterate without chaos
  • produces outputs that hold all the way to production

That’s where BMAD and WDS come in.

Want to see what it looks like in practice, and how a design team can go from prompt burn to a sustainable flow?

All the links you need you will find in the comments

///
Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com

We see it everywhere:

⚡️ Momentum feels real.

It’s slow to get something right.

intention → strategy → specification → execution

The result isn’t a better demo.
It’s fewer do-overs, clearer collaboration, and sustainable delivery.

Speed alone doesn’t win.
Direction does.

posts.title: Årets bästa fest: att implementera AI på designavdelningen 🎈
posts.author: Marten Angner
posts.content_html:
Hej designbyråer, designteam, marknadsavdelningar och kommunikationsavdelningar som jobbar digitalt.

Nu är det hög tid att gå från ord till handling när det gäller AI i designprocessen.

Men frågan är: hur gör man så att det blir roligt, tryggt och faktiskt funkar fantastiskt bra i våra befintliga arbetssätt vardagen?

Vi på Whiteport känner frustrationen hos designchefer, byråchefer och marknadschefer vi pratar med: viljan att komma igång finns, men det är svårt att få grepp om processen.

  • Vad ska in?
  • Vad ska ut?
  • Vad testar vi först?
  • Hur undviker vi ett AI-kaos med 34 verktyg och 0 gemensamma tänk?

AI är en motor, inte en ratt

Att bara kasta in hela gänget i ett kärlekskodningsverktyg och börja spruta ut grejer utan ramar och riktning… det låter kul i fem minuter och känns sedan mest spretigt.

Utan ett gemensamt språk händer två saker:

  1. vissa drar iväg i 180
  2. andra blir stående vid startlinjen

Och då är vi tillbaka på ruta ett, fast med fler flikar öppna.

Varför bry sig alls om AI? Vår designprocess funkar ju rätt ok som det är!

För när AI används med struktur kan designers bli mer produktiva, skapa bättre produkter och samarbeta närmare och smidigare med utvecklingsteam, eller ta fram mer av koden själva.

Istället för att idéer ska sega sig igenom varje lager av organisationen kan teamet tänka snabbare, se behoven tydligare, testa lösningar tidigare och presentera bättre beslutsunderlag.

Och inte vilket beslutsunderlag som helst.

Vi pratar inte bara om realistiska prototyper som går att klicka i och förstå på sekunder, utan också att designers kan fatta bättre beslut på egen hand, och åstadkomma mer färdiga produkter snabbare. Så att beslut fattas på något, har det bättre underlag och är mer verkligt, inte på tolkningar.

Whiteport Design Studio: AI-stöd som bygger vidare på det ni redan gör

Vi har tagit fram WDS, Whiteport Design Studio, ett gratis och open source AI-agentramverk för designers med fokus på att använda AI för att:

  • stödja era befintliga professionella designprocesser
  • snabba upp arbetet i team
  • höja kvaliteten i full skala

Det är inte en “riv upp allt”-grej. Det är en “lägg turbo där det räknas”-grej.

Det känns riskfyllt och abstrakt (helt rimligt)

Att implementera AI kan kännas både läskigt och diffust: många viljor, många behov, många tolkningar.

Och ja, det är utmanande att ifrågasätta en process som trots allt fungerar.

Dessutom: var börjar man?

Men det finns en väg.

Kom igång på ett par veckor. Max.

Vi kan hjälpa er att implementera Whiteport Design Studio i en pilot. Det som ingår:

  • Genomgång av er nuvarande designprocess
  • Kartläggning av styrkor, utmaningar och flaskhalsar
  • Två heldagsworkshops där teamet praktiskt övar, promptar och bygger verkliga resultat redan efter ett par timmar
  • Efteråt stannar vi kvar och supportar i ett par månader så att arbetssätten får fäste och blir en del av vardagen

Målet är enkelt: AI i teamet, på riktigt.

Redan inom någon dag är vi igång. Inom ett par veckor kan ni producera fullt ut, gemensamt, med AI som stöd i processens viktigaste delar. Inte som en gimmick. Som ett arbetssätt.

Allt till fast pris.

Vad väntar du på?

Jobbar du på en designavdelning och vill komma igång: dela det här inlägget med din chef.

///
Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com

Men frågan är: hur gör man så att det blir roligt, tryggt och faktiskt funkar fantastiskt bra i våra befintliga arbetssätt vardagen? Vi på Whiteport känner frustrationen hos designchefer,

social_posts.post_text:
AI i designteamet ska vara kul 🤖✨
Inte ett gravallvarligt transformationsprojekt med 14 styrgrupper och en PowerPoint som aldrig dör.

Med AI kan designers bli:
✅ mer produktiva
✅ skapa bättre produkter
✅ samarbeta närmare med utveckling
✅ testa lösningar snabbare
✅ visa realistiska prototyper som beslutsunderlag

Men… att bara kasta sig in i verktyg och börja spruta ut grejer utan ramar? 😅
Det blir snabbt. Inte alltid bra. Och sällan hållbart.

Vi på Whiteport har därför byggt ett gratis & open source AI-agentramverk för designers:
Whiteport Design Studio. Fokus är att stödja och förbättra era befintliga processer, inte ersätta dem.

Vill ni ta en pilot med oss där ni får fart direkt och bygger riktiga resultat redan efter ett par timmar? 🚀

Kontakta oss via länken i bio.

#design #ux #ui #productdesign #digitaldesign #aidesign #ai #designprocess #prototyping #innovation #teamwork #whiteport

AI i designprocessen ska vara kul. Men det ska också vara styrbart.

Många team fastnar i två extremer: antingen pratar man AI i månader utan att något händer, eller så kastar man sig in i verktyg och börjar producera utan ramar. Det känns snabbt i början, men blir ofta spretigt när alla jobbar på olika sätt och ingen vet vad som är “bra nog”.

Tänk så här: AI är en motor, inte en ratt. Den ger kraft, fart och räckvidd, men ni behöver fortfarande riktning, gemensamt språk och en process som håller.

När AI används med struktur kan designers:

  • ta fram bättre beslutsunderlag snabbare
  • testa lösningar tidigare
  • samarbeta närmare med utveckling
  • bygga mer realistiska prototyper som går att fatta beslut på (på riktigt)

Vi på Whiteport har byggt WDS, Whiteport Design Studio, ett gratis och open source AI-agentramverk för designers. Fokus är att stödja, snabba upp och höja kvaliteten i era befintliga designprocesser, i team och i full skala. Inte “riv upp allt”, utan “lägg turbo där det räknas”.

Vill ni komma igång praktiskt kan vi göra en pilot: vi går igenom er nuvarande process, identifierar styrkor och flaskhalsar, kör två heldagsworkshops där ni bygger verkliga resultat redan efter ett par timmar och stannar kvar med stöd så att arbetssätten får fäste.

Inom dagar är ni igång. Inom veckor kan ni producera fullt ut tillsammans med AI som en naturlig del av jobbet. Fast pris.

Kontakta oss via ett PM.

Årets bästa fest: att implementera AI på designavdelningen 🎈

Hej designbyråer, designteam, marknadsavdelningar och kommunikationsavdelningar som jobbar digitalt.

Nu är det hög tid att gå från ord till handling när det gäller AI i designprocessen.

Men frågan är: hur gör man så att det blir roligt, tryggt och faktiskt funkar i vardagen?

Vi på Whiteport känner igen frustrationen hos byråchefer och marknadschefer: viljan att komma igång finns, men det är svårt att få grepp om processen.

  • Vad ska in?
  • Vad ska ut?
  • Vad testar vi först?
  • Hur undviker vi ett AI-kaos med 34 verktyg och 0 gemensamma arbetssätt?
  • Hur har vi kul här vi gör det?

Fällan Många team fastnar i två extremer: antingen pratar man AI i månader utan att något faktiskt förändras, eller så kastar man sig in i verktyg och börjar producera utan ramar. Det går fort i början, men blir ofta spretigt när alla jobbar på olika sätt, med olika prompts, och ingen vet vad som är “bra nog”.

Principen: AI är en motor, inte en ratt AI ger kraft, fart och räckvidd, men ni behöver fortfarande riktning, gemensamt språk och ett arbetssätt som håller. Annars får ni fler flikar och versioner, men inte mer framdrift.

WDS: Whiteport Design Studio  är gratis/open source)

Vi har byggt ett AI-agentramverk för designers som stödjer era befintliga designprocesser. Inte en “riv upp allt”-grej, utan en “lägg turbo där det räknas”-grej. Metoden är långsiktigt hållbar och går att forma efter hur just ert team jobbar.

Kom igång: pilot i teamet Vi kan hjälpa er att få det att landa i vardagen:

  1. genomgång av er nuvarande process
  2. kartläggning av styrkor och flaskhalsar
  3. två heldagsworkshops där ni promptar, övar och bygger verkliga resultat redan efter ett par timmar
  4. support i ett par månader så att arbetssätten sätter sig

Målet: AI i teamet, på riktigt. Inom dagar är ni igång. Inom veckor kan ni producera fullt ut. Allt till fast pris.

Varför det spelar roll nu AI kan korta vägen mellan idé och beslut. Istället för att idéer ska sega sig igenom lager av organisationen kan teamet se behov, prova alternativ och visa något som känns verkligt, tidigt. Det gör det lättare att säga ja, nej eller ”inte än” tryggt. Och ja, det kan kännas riskfyllt och abstrakt, därför börjar vi praktiskt och småskaligt.

Kontakta oss på hello@whiteport.com.

#aidesign #uxdesign #productdesign #designprocess #prototyping #innovation #ai #whiteport

posts.title: Do you avoid thinking in digital projects? Think again!
posts.author: Marten Angner
posts.content_html:
Development has become insanely cheap.

With AI you can accomplish a lot of digital product output in very little time. You can spin up a prototype, generate UI, scaffold code, and ship something that looks “real” in the time it used to take to book a conference room!

This accessibility is genuinely flipping exciting because more people get to build, test, and learn in public.

But, when doing gets cheap, people are tempted to skip the thinking.

Why spend days on strategy when you can build in hours?

Here’s the twist.

Strategic thinking powered by AI is getting lightning fast too. ⚡

When you combine a sound design process with a set of trained AI agents, the thinking part stops being a slow, expensive “phase” and becomes a guided conversation that surfaces intent, clarifies tradeoffs, and turns fuzzy ideas into decisions you can actually build from.

Thinking is saving a lot of headache further down the line!

Teams are producing more output than ever, but the risk is that the output becomes the work, because it is so easy to generate something that looks finished while the hard questions remain unasked.

And then it bites.

Late pivots. Rewrites. Stakeholder whiplash. Products that ship and still miss. So yes, doing is cheap. 

But, but, ignorance has an enormous cost down the line!

Strategy with no compromises!

A sound design process begins by getting clear on who we’re helping, what they want to achieve, what’s true about the context, what success looks like, and what we should not build.

WDS accelerates that work by having agents ask the right questions at the right time so you don’t drift, you don’t forget the basics, and you don’t settle for the first idea that happens to render nicely. Those questions pull the best ideas out of you, and the answers become strategic documentation as you go, which means you don’t only end up with a strategy, you end up with a reusable starting point.

Pressure.

In a good way.

Your strategic goldmine that keeps on giving!

The documentation is not just a document that alligns stakeholders and a development team. It also make up the back bone of all your agent conversations. As it turns out your WDS Strategic documents the best possible meta prompt for the project.

Every time you restart an agent conversation you can snap back to the same direction, the same intent, the same definitions, instead of re-explaining your project from scratch and hoping the AI remembers the important parts.

Alignment.

Every time.

There is no longer any reason to skip the thinking.

Invitation: WDS Session 3 (Let’s get going)

WDS Session 3 is for every designer on the planet who wants a practical guide to getting started with WDS, where we’ll show how to begin with strategy (fast), turn it into documentation (automatic), and use that as the foundation for execution (stable).

Hands-on.

Concrete.

We’ll cover:

  • how to start the WDS flow with strategy instead of output
  • how the agents surface the strongest direction through questions
  • how the documentation becomes a reusable meta prompt
  • how to go strategy → scope → execution without losing clarity

Session 3 details (fill in):

  • When: [DATE], [TIME]
  • Where: [LOCATION / LINK]

Join: [SIGNUP LINK]

///
Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com

Du kan skissa UI, generera kod och få något som ser färdigt ut på en eftermiddag.

Och därför hoppar många över det viktigaste.

Tänket.

Grejen är att strategiskt tänkande med AI också går blixtsnabbt, särskilt när du får hjälp av tränade agenter som ställer rätt frågor och skriver dokumentationen åt dig.

WDS Session 3: praktisk guide för att komma igång.

Kontakta oss via länken i bio.

Att programmera saker och få fram något som ser färdigt ut har blivit billigt, vilket är fantastiskt… men det gör också att många hoppar över tänket och går direkt på output.

Det folk missar är att strategiskt tänkande med AI också kan vara blixtsnabbt när du har rätt upplägg.

Whiteport Design Studio börjar som alla sunda designprocesser: med strategi, men den här gången är tänket superladdat av AI-agenter som ställer rätt frågor, drar fram de bästa idéerna och skriver dokumentationen åt dig.

WDS Session 3 är en praktisk guide där alla designers är välkomna.

Kontakta oss via ett PM.

Development has become insanely cheap.

With AI, you can prototype, generate UI, scaffold code, and ship something that looks real in hours. What used to take weeks now happens before you book a meeting.

That’s exciting.
It’s also risky.

When doing gets cheap, teams are tempted to skip the thinking.

Why spend days on strategy when you can build in hours?

The overlooked shift: strategic thinking powered by AI is getting lightning fast too.

Combine a sound design process with trained AI agents, and strategy stops being a slow, abstract phase. It becomes a guided conversation that clarifies intent, surfaces tradeoffs, and turns fuzzy ideas into decisions you can actually build from.

That thinking saves real pain downstream:
• Late pivots
• Rewrites
• Stakeholder whiplash
• Products that ship and still miss

Yes, doing is cheap.
But ignorance is still expensive.

WDS starts with the fundamentals: who we’re helping, what success looks like, what’s true about the context—and what we should not build. Agents builds ask the questions that matter and the answers become strategic documentation as you go.

That documentation doesn’t just align teams.
It becomes the backbone of every future agent conversation—the project’s multipurpose meta prompt.

Alignment. Every time.

Invitation: WDS Session 3 — Let’s get going

A hands-on session for designers who want to build what matters star with clarity of thinking.

posts.title: Whiteport Design Studio Course: 00 – Getting Started
posts.author: Camilla Andersson
posts.content_html:
The course begins with Module 00 – Getting Started. In just a few lessons (~30 minutes), you’ll learn how to set up your environment, install an IDE, and get comfortable with GitHub. This module ensures you’re ready to dive into Module 01 – Why WDS Matters, where you’ll explore the paradigm shift that makes specifications central, not peripheral, to design. Together, these first modules set the foundation for creating your four deliverables and thriving in the AI era.

The designer’s crossroads

Designers today face a choice: continue producing outputs AI can replicate, or become linchpins whose creative thinking is indispensable. Traditional workflows—mockups, handoffs, repeat—are increasingly replaceable by AI.

Whiteport Design Studio (WDS) is a free, open-source course designed to transform designers from executing tasks into creating enduring systems. Created by Mårten Angner, founder of Whiteport (Sweden), WDS integrates AI agents to capture and preserve your design thinking, turning design decisions into immortal specifications, not lost mockups.

What you’ll learn: The four deliverables

The course guides you to produce four outputs that immortalize your creative brilliance:

  1. Project Brief – Your strategic foundation: vision, goals, stakeholders, and constraints.
  2. Trigger Map – Align user needs and business impact; prioritize features with purpose.
  3. Scenario Specifications – Capture every design decision with AI as your creative partner.
  4. Design System Foundation – Extract tokens, patterns, and reusable components to scale your work.

These deliverables shift you from being a mockup maker to a strategic designer whose thinking lasts.

Getting started

  1. Start with Module 00 – Getting Started on the WDS GitHub repository.
  2. Proceed to Module 01 – Why WDS Matters to understand the design paradigm shift.
  3. Follow the course step-by-step to create your four deliverables (~10 hours total).
  4. Join the community via BMad Discord or GitHub Discussions for feedback and support.

Cost: Free and open-source. AI credits are optional, ~$15–20/month for active use.

The choice is yours

Whiteport Design Studio isn’t just a methodology—it’s a career transformation.

Design becomes the specification. Specification becomes the product. Code is just the printout.

Start with Module 00, follow the course, and choose the path of the indispensable designer, whose creativity and strategic thinking cannot be replaced.

YouTube course link: https://youtu.be/qYPYx01YLUc

posts.title: Designers know how to make product people love
posts.author: Camilla Andersson
posts.content_html:
This is what changes everything.

In WDS Session 1, we explored what happens when you’re forced to explain your ideas.
Component by component, decision by decision.

When your product is generated from a clear specification, change becomes simple.
You don’t hunt through 500 lines of code.
You adjust the intent.
You reshuffle decisions.
You regenerate.

No spaghetti code.
No fragile fixes.
Just a system that stays coherent because it’s rebuilt from clarity every time.

This is what happens when code becomes a projection of strategy – not the other way around.

WDS brings strategic design thinking together with AI agent technology – It’s free and open source!
https://whiteport.com/products/wds/

posts.title: A 9-step masterclass in digital strategy: the pain, the pathway, the payoff!
posts.author: Marten Angner
posts.content_html:
Strategy is one of those words that gets tossed around like confetti, and then, with straight faces, expected to carry the weight of a company on its tiny paper shoulders. At Whiteport Design Studio, we treat strategy as a tool with a sharp edge: useful, deliberate, and not something you wave around like a buzzword bingo contest.

Working with digital strategy requires courage, because it asks you to stay in the problem while you explore the solution space with an open mind, methodically gathering signals and insights until you hit that breakthrough moment where everything clicks and the path forward suddenly becomes obvious. Not as the first idea you fall in love with in an instant, but as the outcome of feeding your brain enough relevant information and giving your team (and your AI assistants) the right constraints to work with.

Strategic thinking isn’t here to slow you down with stupid questions.

It’s here to focus your energy on what actually matters.

So get passionate about this part of project preparation like the whole project depends on it, because it does.

Because it does.

Overview: The Whiteport Strategy Thinking Loop

Here’s the map before the hike, the checklist before the chaos, the loop we return to whenever a team feels either stuck or suspiciously confident.

  1. Describe the desired state = vision. Make it concrete enough that people can disagree productively, because vague visions create polite meetings and sloppy decisions.
  2. Describe the current state = the challenge. What is true right now, what hurts, what is wasted, and what is being assumed without evidence.
  3. Who are we trying to create value for? Anchor your thinking with target group analysis, and define what your audience is actually trying to achieve.
  4. Stay in the problem on purpose. Sit with the tension long enough for understanding to form, because the first relief you feel is usually just the first shortcut.
  5. Interrogate the “why.” Use something like 5 Whys to dig past symptoms, so you don’t build an expensive band-aid and call it innovation.
  6. Generate options. Document the first idea, then force alternatives into existence, because comparison is how you earn certainty.
  7. Anchor to objectives. Rank the business goals and pressure-test each option against the top metric first, then the next, until the weak ideas politely fall apart.
  8. Plan in phases. Milestones, assumptions, risks, and checkpoints that reality can slap without destroying the whole project.
  9. Learn with real people. Build the smallest thing, show it to at least 5 users and 1 domain expert, then decide: pivot or persevere.

Then we loop.

And we get better.

1. Vision first, or you are just orbiting

A vision is a clear understanding of a desired state, expressed in a way that people can actually picture, argue with, and rally around, even when they’re tired, busy, or slightly allergic to change. It’s not a vague wish. Not a mood board. Not a slogan that looks great on a wall and means nothing in a meeting.

A strategy without a vision is going in circles.

You can move. You can be busy. You can even feel productive.

But you are still spinning.

So when you have your vision, it’s time to think about the journey to get there. This is where strategy stops being a buzzword and becomes a choice with consequences.

2. Strategy is the route from current to desired state

Strategy is the pathway we choose from the current state to the desired state, and the word choose matters because it implies trade-offs, constraints, and the uncomfortable truth that not everything can be priority number one.

A strategy includes which approaches we should use, which methods we’ll rely on, how we maximize our strengths, how we avoid pitfalls, and how we reduce resistance, both technical and human.

One common mistake is talking about strategy in plural.

Many people talk about all the strategies they have to reach an objective, when in practice you can only have one, because strategy is the coherent route you commit to, not a buffet you sample depending on mood.

The strategy can have several parts.

But it is always one.

And still, we’re not moving just yet.

At this point, we’re still at the start, staying with the challenge, and that can feel frustrating if you came into the room hoping for a shiny solution by lunch.

Good.

3. Staying in the problem: remember, pain is temporary, regret is forever

Almost every workshop method known to mankind embodies “staying in the problem,” because as you put effort into viewing, clarifying, and burning grey matter on the current challenge, your understanding deepens, your assumptions get exposed, and your team gradually stops talking past each other.

You are not wasting time here.

You are buying clarity.

And clarity is what makes execution fast later.

But here’s the problem.

Staying in the problem is painful.

As soon as you’re in the problem, you’re suffering, and it doesn’t matter whether the “problem” is a missed opportunity or an urgent issue, because your nervous system experiences both as tension, uncertainty, and the sense that something is not yet safe.

And while you’re in that state, your brain starts grasping for release.

Any release.

Which leads to the biggest pitfall in human thinking.

It turns out that very few people can operate effectively in low certainty. Experienced consultants get good at it through training.

You can too.

Falling in love with the first idea

Amateurs leave the problem at the first whim, and inexperienced thinkers run with whatever comes to mind, because an idea feels like oxygen when you’ve been underwater in uncertainty.

The danger is that the first idea is rarely the best idea.

It’s just the most emotionally relieving.

And emotions are talented liars when they’re trying to get you out of discomfort.

That’s why companies like Toyota are famous for forcing solutions through structured problem definition. The “5 Whys” practice, for example, isn’t about being slow or bureaucratic. It’s about documenting your thinking so you can prove you didn’t stop at the first convenient explanation, and it creates a cultural barrier against premature certainty.

Experienced consultants learn to be comfortable in the problem.

You should too.

But there’s also a trap on the other side of the same mountain.

4. Finding out “who wants what” in your project’s solution space

Knowing your customer is a fantastic way to create creative boundaries for your thinking, because clear people-thinking creates better product-thinking. When you define your target groups and get clear on what they actually want, you put everyone’s mind on high alert for good ideas, because you’ve turned guessing into observing.

We usually do this in two steps.

First, create your ICP (Ideal Customer Profile). Define the type of company, organization, or customer segment that gives you the best return on effort, and capture their pains and opportunities by speaking to what makes their world unique.

Second, identify the actual type of person you’re doing business with, because all business is done between people. Define a persona and clearly list what they want, what they fear, what they’re trying to achieve, and what they’re trying to avoid.

At Whiteport Design Studio, we use Trigger Mapping to identify both positive and negative driving forces:

  • Positive: goals, wishes, dreams, ambitions
  • Negative: fears, aversions, nightmares, and what they want to avoid

5. Analysis paralysis: when “why” becomes a hiding place

Staying in the “why” for too long is also a mistake, because analysis can become a socially acceptable way to avoid commitment, and commitment is where accountability begins.

Many lengthy reports have been written about issues without any solution being proposed, not because nobody had an idea, but because proposing a solution gets your name attached to it, and in low-trust environments people become reluctant to put their name on anything that might fail.

So the team keeps analyzing.

And analyzing.

And analyzing.

Toyota has a brutal antidote to this: A3 thinking (a one-page forced decision).

A business case is expressed on one A3 paper in landscape.

Left side: define the problem.

Right side: propose the solution.

You can’t hide behind page 48.

One page forces a decision.

6. Progress from strategy to solution focus!

When we have a clear understanding of the challenge, a defined vision, and a strategy, we start gathering clues for a solution, and those clues come from friction points in the current system, business objectives you can’t ignore, constraints you must respect, and patterns you notice when you stop trying to escape discomfort.

Sometimes you get the solution first, and strategic work is used to challenge that initial idea so you can keep what is strong and discard what is wishful thinking.

Other times you move methodically through the strategic steps until the pieces fall into place.

Either way, the process is the same.

Finding the right solution.

How to not marry your first idea

To avoid falling for the first idea that pops into your mind, force yourself to document it, generate alternatives, and compare options, even when it feels like overkill, because that friction is the price of not building the wrong thing with great confidence.

This is a skill.

And sometimes it requires balls of steel when the whole team is screaming to “just go with whatever is on the table,” because urgency can be real and still be misleading.

A good way to generate stronger alternatives is to list all the business objectives behind the solution, prioritize them, and ask what would happen if you were only allowed to deliver value to the most important metric.

That constraint reveals what is essential.

Everything else starts to wobble.

As you go down the list, you’ll often see that your darling idea isn’t the most practical, or that it only works if the world behaves nicely.

The world rarely behaves nicely.

The taboo question: should we live with the problem?

Another assumption is that the challenge is just one problem, or that it’s solvable with one solution, but reality tends to be messier than our PowerPoints.

What about the cost of the fix?

The disruption?

The maintenance?

The second-order effects that show up six months later when nobody remembers why you built the thing?

Are we really sure the juice is worth the squeeze?

Sometimes, by staying in the problem long enough, we realize that living with the issue might be less costly than trying to solve it.

That is not surrender.

That is strategy.

7. Planning: now we move, but the plan is not the point

Once we have a solution that aligns with vision and strategy, it’s time to plan, which means we take the roadmap implied by the strategy and break it into phases and milestones, so execution becomes a sequence of decisions rather than a foggy marathon.

Planning is where we translate intent into coordination, and it’s also where we surface risks early, because a plan is basically a list of assumptions wearing a suit.

The planning is the point.

The plan is nothing.

Napoleon Bonaparte is often credited with the idea that no plan survives contact with the enemy, and the reason is simple: reality gets a vote, and reality votes early and often.

So plans must be flexible.

One eye on the plan.

One eye on reality.

As we work, we gather evidence whether our plan is working or not, and evidence should be allowed to change our minds.

If we’re brave enough to look.

The first steps always feel like failure

In the beginning of a project it often feels like you’re getting nowhere, because most energy is spent setting everything up, everything looks like a mess, and everyone is running around like headless chickens with project boards and good intentions.

It’s easy to decide the solution was stupid, the market isn’t ready, or the idea is wrong.

And stop.

But you need to stick with the plan long enough to get factual data outside your emotional state.

Feelings are not reality.

The other mistake: pushing through obvious red flags

On the flip side, pushing through while evidence stacks up that the solution is failing is also a mistake.

Downplaying skepticism and dismissing different opinions is how you drive a project off a cliff with a confident smile.

Plenty of ventures have failed because consequences were ignored for too long, not because people were stupid, but because the social cost of saying “this isn’t working” felt higher than continuing.

So what’s the cure?

8. Checkpoints: talk to real people

We plan for checkpoints where we talk to real people, because feedback from reality is the only antidote to the fantasy that lives in our heads.

In software development, best practice is to make something simple, bring it to the target group, and learn.

The art isn’t just building the thing.

It’s asking the right questions without leading the witness.

A useful rule of thumb:

Show your thing to at least 5 users.

And 1 domain expert.

Then let the feedback actually sink in.

Lean Startup has a framework that captures this ritual beautifully. The point isn’t shipping for the sake of shipping. The point is validated learning.

The cycle looks like this:

  1. Define a minimum viable product (MVP)
  2. Produce it in the cheapest way possible
  3. Evaluate what we learned internally
  4. Show it to real people from the target group
  5. Evaluate the feedback and take notice
  6. Ask the big question: pivot or persevere

And there is our old friend again.

Discomfort.

Staying in the moment, letting everyone share their view, and asking for feedback is painful, because it feels like it takes too much time, it’s hard to find the right people, and we already suspect our solution is bad anyway, so why do we need other people to tell us?

The list of rationalizations to skip usability testing is endless.

But they all boil down to the same point.

Fear.

Fear of what people will say.

Fear of looking foolish.

Fear of having built a bad product.

When it comes to designing interactive products, “pain is temporary, regret is forever” actually has merit, because the pain of feedback happens now, while the regret of launching the wrong thing tends to arrive later.

Quietly.

With invoices.

AI is speeding up building, but keeping you ignorant

Here’s what worries me.

AI and “love-coding” tools have changed the texture of software development, and they can de-sensitize us to methodical thinking because they make it possible to produce something that looks finished before we’ve earned the right to believe we understand the problem.

Why plan when you can build the product in a couple of hours?

When thinking takes longer than building, why put two thoughts together when you could just ship and see what happens?

Because speed can be a trap.

When our thinking is clear, AI doesn’t have to make assumptions.

And when AI doesn’t have to guess, the team doesn’t have to clean up guesswork disguised as output.

We know what to build.

We become curious about the effect of our work.

And together, we build in accordance with a shared understanding of the world, which means less thrash, fewer debates about symptoms, and more energy spent on what actually matters.

And there is no better feeling.

How Whiteport can help

Whiteport is a digital agency based in Stockholm, Sweden. We help teams get clarity in their thinking, sharpen their solutions, and turn early ideas into design that can actually fly.

Need help with analysis, usability testing, digital strategy, social media, UX design, visual design, or production?

We’re here.

Whiteport Design Studio

Whiteport Design Studio is not a slide deck you admire and then ignore.

It’s a disciplined sequence that keeps teams honest while still moving forward:

Vision that people actually want.

One coherent strategy that chooses a route.

The courage to stay in the problem without falling in love too early.

The humility to test assumptions with real people.

Sketches elevated into interfaces, and interfaces turned into specifications your developers can actually use.

Plans that bend when reality speaks.

Evidence-driven decisions to pivot or persevere.

That is what strategy looks like when it is alive.

Not busy.

Alive.

///
Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com

posts.title: WDS Sessions 2: Speed isn’t a strategy!
posts.author: Camilla Andersson
posts.content_html:
Right now, designers everywhere are in a fever-like love affair with vibe-coding.

The promise is intoxicating: a couple of prompts, and suddenly you have screens that look finished. But then reality shows up, wearing a spreadsheet. Pet projects turn into costly and disappointing products.

Experienced CX, UI, and UX designers feel it first: speed can’t make up for a lack of sound thinking. Burning tokens at lightning pace to ship a product nobody wants is not a viable path to success. AI or not, a bad idea is still a bad idea.

Somewhere along the way, digital strategy became the child we threw out with the bathwater the moment our first prompt produced something shiny.

What design strategy is (and why it matters again)

Design strategy is a high-level, intentional plan that bridges business goals with user needs. It aligns aesthetics and function with objectives.

In short: it’s the art of avoiding costly and embarrassing apps that nobody wants.

Strategy isn’t a mood. It’s decisions:

  • Who are we serving?
  • What problem are we solving (in their words)?
  • What outcomes matter, and how will we measure them?
  • What are we not building, on purpose?

AI doesn’t save you from weak thinking.

AI accelerates you.

Whiteport Design Studio: build strategy right in with the code!

Whiteport Design Studio (WDS) is an end-to-end design workflow that uses trained AI agents to help designers navigate the strategic dimension of any digital project.

You run WDS in an IDE, like Cursor, WIndsurf or Google Antigravity. This achieves a number of things, You stop using documents that you bring between apps and servers. Here you work, save and share your work with others in the same way developers work with code. 

The goal isn’t to “prompt harder.” It’s to create structured product artifacts that capture intent, decisions, constraints, and priorities so AI can build with precision and your team can change course without breaking everything.

Then when the strategy is made

If specifications become the new code, strategy becomes the foundation.

This thursday we will talk more about Digital Strategy

Learn how to use Trigger Mapping to turn messy reality into a clear digital plan. Stop generating prototypes that don’t connect to outcomes and start building the right thing before building fast.

What You’ll Learn:

  • What digital strategy is (and isn’t) – no fluff, just usable clarity
  • How Trigger Mapping works – map moments that create action and opportunity
  • How to avoid “AI-powered wandering” and connect to real outcomes
  • How designers can lead strategy using WDS agents

posts.content_raw:
Right now, designers everywhere are in a fever-like love affair with vibe-coding.

The promise is intoxicating: a couple of prompts, and suddenly you have screens that look finished. But then reality shows up, wearing a spreadsheet. Pet projects turn into costly and disappointing products.

Experienced CX, UI, and UX designers feel it first: speed can’t make up for a lack of sound thinking. Burning tokens at lightning pace to ship a product nobody wants is not a viable path to success. AI or not, a bad idea is still a bad idea.

Somewhere along the way, digital strategy became the child we threw out with the bathwater the moment our first prompt produced something shiny.

What design strategy is (and why it matters again)

Design strategy is a high-level, intentional plan that bridges business goals with user needs. It aligns aesthetics and function with objectives.

In short: it’s the art of avoiding costly and embarrassing apps that nobody wants.

Strategy isn’t a mood. It’s decisions:

  • Who are we serving?
  • What problem are we solving (in their words)?
  • What outcomes matter, and how will we measure them?
  • What are we not building, on purpose?

AI doesn’t save you from weak thinking.

AI accelerates you.

Whiteport Design Studio (WDS) is an end-to-end design workflow that uses trained AI agents to help designers navigate the strategic dimension of any digital project.

The goal isn’t to “prompt harder.” It’s to create structured product artifacts that capture intent, decisions, constraints, and priorities so AI can build with precision and your team can change course without breaking everything.

Then when the strategy is made

If specifications become the new code, strategy becomes the foundation.

Learn how to use Trigger Mapping to turn messy reality into a clear digital plan. Stop generating prototypes that don’t connect to outcomes and start building the right thing before building fast.

  • How Trigger Mapping works – map moments that create action and opportunity

posts.title: Who is better suited to make sense of the product
posts.author: Camilla Andersson
posts.content_html:
AI doesn’t replace design thinking.
It reveals it.

In WDS Session 1, we explored what happens when you’re forced to explain your ideas.
Component by component, decision by decision.

That’s where the shift happens:
Your sketch gets smarter.
Your intent becomes clearer.
And the “code” becomes a direct projection of what you actually mean.

This is why CX, UX, and UI designers are uniquely positioned in an AI-driven world.
Because we’re trained to turn intention into outcomes.

This is where the magic really happens. 💫

WDS brings strategic design thinking together with AI agent technology – It’s free and open source!
https://whiteport.com/products/wds/

There is no choice anymore. Designers need to learn AI in 2026! WDS is your chance!

posts.title: Vad bygger du i mellandagarna?
posts.author: Marten Angner
posts.content_html:
Det är något speciellt med tiden mellan jul och nyår. Kalendern har tappat greppet om verkligheten, inkorgen är (för en gångs skull) lite mindre bossig, och världen känns som att den går på halvfart. Som om allt är inbäddat i bomull.

För mig är mellandagarna ett sorts tillstånd. En liten frizon där jag får bygga saker jag inte hinner när vardagen spelar trummor på min panna.

Jag gillar att jobba då, för att jag jobbar annorlunda. Mindre “nu ska vi leverera”, mer “nu ska vi utforska”. Det är som att hjärnan får gå runt i mjukisbyxor och peka på idéer i hyllan: “Den där! Den har vi inte lekt med på länge.”

I år bygger jag vidare på ett open source-projekt: AI-agenter för designers

Den här julen har jag fortsatt att bygga på mitt projekt att skapa AI-agenter för designers, open source på nätet.

Och det fina är att det här inte handlar om “AI som skriver kod åt dig”. Det handlar om att designers kan få hjälp att tänka, strukturera och formulera det som annars blir en enda lång, tröttsam promptmaraton.

För om vi ska vara ärliga: Att prompta fram en hel produkt är ofta som att försöka bygga ett kök genom att viska önskemål till en snabb men lättstressad byggare. Det blir… något. Men sällan det man menade. 😅

Det jag har byggt i mellandagarna: flöden som gör idéer mer byggbara

Jag har jobbat vidare på Whiteport Design Studio, mitt AI-agentramverk för designers. Projektet är open source och innehåller ett end-to-end-flöde som hjälper designers att:

  • få klarhet i projektets målgrupp, målsättning och ramar innan du bygger
  • lägga upp scenarios och sidor (så man inte börjar med “en app” utan med en faktisk situation)
  • skissa ut idéer på ett sätt som går att förstå och vidareutveckla
  • skapa konceptspecar som kan ersätta timmar och dagar av promptande när man ska utveckla med AI

Det är där jag tycker magin händer: när vi flyttar fokus från “skriv kod åt mig” till “hjälp mig skapa digitala produkter som känns magiska för användaren.”

En bra konceptspecifikation är som en karta med höjdkurvor och vägskyltar. Den säger inte bara vart vi ska, utan också var det är brant och var det är sankmark. Den gör det lättare att ta beslut, lättare att bygga, och mycket lättare att ändra sig utan att allt rasar.

Varför mellandagarna är perfekta för sånt här

För att det är då man faktiskt kan stanna upp och fråga:

Vad är det vi försöker åstadkomma egentligen? Inte vad vi ska bygga, utan vad som ska bli bättre för någon.

När tempot sjunker blir det tydligare vilka idéer som är “kul i stunden” och vilka som faktiskt har bäring. Och jag tror att det är exakt den sortens tänk som behövs nu, när det är så lätt att bygga snabbt.

Vi lever i en tid där du kan vibekoda fram en prototyp på en eftermiddag. Det är otroligt. Men varje sekund du bygger på fel sak är också en sekund du flyttar fokus från att bygga något som spelar roll.

Så mellandagarna blir min motrörelse. Mitt lilla “mät två gånger, såga en gång”.

Och du då?

Vad bygger du när världen är lite tystare? Bygger du kod, skisser, musik, relationer, vanor, ett nytt sätt att jobba?

Skriv gärna och berätta. Jag är genuint nyfiken, och jag tror vi behöver fler samtal som börjar i skaparlust istället för “vad levererar du just nu”? 

///
Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com

Vad bygger du i mellandagarna? 🎄🛠️

Jag älskar tiden mellan jul och nyår. Världen går på halvfart, inkorgen är mindre högljudd, och man hinner bygga sånt som annars aldrig får plats.

I år bygger jag vidare på mitt open source-projekt: AI-agenter för designers. Flöden som hjälper dig att: ✨ skapa scenarios och sidor ✨ skissa idéer så de blir begripliga ✨ skriva konceptspecar som kan ersätta timmar av promptande i AI-utveckling

Det är inte “AI som gör jobbet”. Det är AI som hjälper dig tänka tydligare.

Vad bygger du just nu, berätta i kommentarerna? 👇

#design #ux #productdesign #ai #aidesign #opensource #digitalproduct #prototyping #strategy #buildinpublic #designsystems

Vad bygger du i mellandagarna? 🎄

Jag tycker så mycket om tiden mellan jul och nyår. Tempot sjunker, världen blir lite tystare och man får plats att bygga saker som annars alltid hamnar “sen”.

Den här julen bygger jag vidare på mitt open source-projekt: AI-agenter för designers. Jag har skapat flöden för scenarios, sidor, skisser och konceptspecar, så att man kan ersätta timmar (och dagar) av promptande när man ska utveckla med AI.

Vad bygger du just nu? Berätta  gärna i kommentarerna, jag är så nyfiken!

Vad bygger du i mellandagarna?

Jag gillar verkligen tiden mellan jul och nyår. När tempot sjunker händer något med tänkandet: man bygger inte bara snabbare, man bygger klarare.

Den här julen har jag fortsatt mitt open source-projekt: AI-agenter för designers. Fokus är att skapa flöden som hjälper designers att: • sätta upp scenarios och sidor • skissa idéer på ett sätt som går att utveckla vidare • skapa konceptspecar som kan ersätta timmar och dagar av promptande i AI-driven utveckling

Jag tror att nästa stora produktivitetshopp inte bara handlar om att generera kod snabbare, utan om att skapa bättre produktartefakter innan vi bygger.

posts.title: First you think. Then you Vibe!
posts.author: Marten Angner
posts.content_html:
AI makes it ridiculously fast to build things.  But, what if, now with AI, you just built the 𝑤𝑟𝑜𝑛𝑔 thing… faster? 🤔
Vibe-coding tools hand you that instant dopamine: “It runs!” ✅

But here’s the uncomfortable question:

What if you just built the wrong thing… faster?

What if your idea would have been twice as good if you’d spent one hour talking to real humans first? Domain experts. Users. The people who will actually live with the outcome.

Most workshop methods under the sun have the same hidden agenda:
Stay in the problem long enough to understand it.

A carpenter measures twice and cuts once.
A doctor diagnoses before prescribing.
But in the world of “vibe”… we often build before we think. And strategy is becoming a bit of a lost art in 2026.

If it’s a hobby project, fine. Ship, learn, laugh, repeat. 🎈
But if it’s a business-critical product inside a company, a headless sprint without user insight can get… expensive. Not just in money, but in trust, adoption, and momentum.

And honestly: talking to real people is underrated fun.
Most people like being asked for their expertise. It’s validating, energising, and it often turns into a surprisingly good conversation. Plus, you walk away with something no tool can generate for you: context. Nuance. Sharp edges. The “oh wow, I didn’t think of that” moments.

Built in to the Whiteport Design Studio, you find a Trigger Mapping module with Workshops hosted by our Analyst agent Saga and templates for using the result of the workshop to mold the product! This is an exercise we reccomend you before you build, regardless what tool you would like to use to build your product!

That’s why I’m hosting WDS Session 2: Strategy in practise with WDS.
We’ll talk about Digital Strategy and simple, practical ways to ask the right questions and capture the answers, so you can fit your strategy into a complete one-slide foundation before you design and build.

🗓️ Thursday, January 15, 2026

Hi, I am Mårten, I am the founder of Whiteport, a Digital Agency from Stockholm. We make Apps, sites and E-commerse applications with the help of AI Agents! And we love it. Email me at marten@angner.se or find us on www.whiteport.com
posts.content_raw:
AI makes it ridiculously fast to build things.  But, what if, now with AI, you just built the 𝑤𝑟𝑜𝑛𝑔 thing… faster? 🤔
Vibe-coding tools hand you that instant dopamine: “It runs!” ✅

But here’s the uncomfortable question:

What if you just built the wrong thing… faster?

What if your idea would have been twice as good if you’d spent one hour talking to real humans first? Domain experts. Users. The people who will actually live with the outcome.

Most workshop methods under the sun have the same hidden agenda:
Stay in the problem long enough to understand it.

A carpenter measures twice and cuts once.
A doctor diagnoses before prescribing.
But in the world of “vibe”… we often build before we think. And strategy is becoming a bit of a lost art in 2026.

If it’s a hobby project, fine. Ship, learn, laugh, repeat. 🎈
But if it’s a business-critical product inside a company, a headless sprint without user insight can get… expensive. Not just in money, but in trust, adoption, and momentum.

And honestly: talking to real people is underrated fun.
Most people like being asked for their expertise. It’s validating, energising, and it often turns into a surprisingly good conversation. Plus, you walk away with something no tool can generate for you: context. Nuance. Sharp edges. The “oh wow, I didn’t think of that” moments.

Built in to the Whiteport Design Studio, you find a Trigger Mapping module with Workshops hosted by our Analyst agent Saga and templates for using the result of the workshop to mold the product! This is an exercise we reccomend you before you build, regardless what tool you would like to use to build your product!

That’s why I’m hosting WDS Session 2: Strategy in practise with WDS.
We’ll talk about Digital Strategy and simple, practical ways to ask the right questions and capture the answers, so you can fit your strategy into a complete one-slide foundation before you design and build.

Webinar details
🗓️ Thursday, January 15, 2026
📍 Online
🎙️ Host: Mårten Angner

⏱️ 17:00 – 18:00 Stockholm (CET)
⏱️ 8AM – 9AM Pacific (LA)
⏱️ 9AM – 10AM Mountain (MT)
⏱️ 10AM–11AM Central (CT)
⏱️ 11AM – 12PM Eastern (ET)
⏱️ 4PM – 5PM UK / Ireland (GMT)
⏱️ 18:00–19:00 Eastern Europe (EET)

Registration link:
https://whiteport.com/blog/wds-sessions-2-strategy-in-practise-with-wds/

Did you miss Session 1? Here is the recording:

posts.title: Welcome to the WDS Designers Community 2026
posts.author: Camilla Andersson
posts.content_html:
Designers, here me out. we all just have to embrace AI in 2026. There is no way around it. But, the good news is that we can choose how we do it💫

I know, the world has gone crazy. We designer have worked for decades to come up with design methods that work in creating digital products. Design Thinking and User Centered Design is great methods, but now, all of a sudden, everyone are just expected to jump in to some stupid vibe coding tool and churn out things.

We designers know, that building something without structure, strategy and dialogs with the end users is a non starter.

When we designers are not vibing with Vibe coding and take a step back, the world loses out on our collective experience, and this is going to be a disaster for interactive products.

This is why I founded WDS, Whiteport Design Studio, a free and open source AI agent module for the BMad method, that amplifies your existing design process instead of trying to avoid them.

Check out our new WDS webpage to find out exactly what WDS is, and how to learn it. When you are there, make sure to sign up to the WDS News Letter to not miss a single update!
https://whiteport.com/products/wds/

Happy New Years!

///
